ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for salted vegetable with beef stir-fry; A0CKP (stir-fried mixed dish with meat and vegetables) used as the closest defensible parent anchor. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific Singaporean Chinese preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of stir-fried salted vegetable with beef as a side dish in a Singaporean meal is approximately 150g, often shared alongside rice and other dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Salted Vegetable with Beef — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Stir Fried Salted Vegetable with Beef (cr_id F14080054), confidence 0.95. All core macros and available micronutrients are sourced from this record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Vegetable and Meat Dishes — Analogous Estimate Based on Low-Carbohydrate Stir-Fried Vegetable Dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried salted vegetable with beef. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on analogous low-carbohydrate stir-fried vegetable and meat dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is 3.3g/100g; glycemic load per 150g serving = (15 × 3.3 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.74, rounded to 0.7. The very low carbohydrate content means glycemic impact is minimal regardless of GI category.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 1061mg per 100g, driven by salted preserved vegetables and soy/oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; a 150g serving delivers approximately 1592mg sodium, exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2000mg in a single serving, and portion reduction or requesting a low-sodium preparation is strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content at 3.3g per 100g with moderate protein at 7.2g supporting glycaemic moderation
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate load; however, sodium content is extremely high and should be monitored, and the dish should be paired with a high-fibre staple to support overall glycaemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high sodium at 1061mg/100g combined with saturated fat from beef raises cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this dish due to its very high sodium content and saturated fat from beef, both of which contribute to elevated blood pressure and atherogenic risk; if consumed, portion size should be kept to 50–75g and paired with potassium-rich foods to partially offset sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef provides arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokines; high sodium worsens joint swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish as beef is a primary dietary trigger for inflammatory cytokine amplification (TNF-alpha, IL-6), and the very high sodium content further exacerbates joint swelling and tissue inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Salted Vegetable with Beef (咸菜炒牛肉, Xian Cai Chao Niu Rou) is a traditional Singaporean Chinese home-cooked dish consisting of preserved salted mustard greens (咸菜) stir-fried with sliced beef and aromatics such as garlic and ginger, typically seasoned with soy sauce and oyster sauce. The dish is cooked over high heat in a wok until fragrant, resulting in a savoury, slightly salty flavour profile with a tender-crisp texture from the preserved vegetables and a chewy, umami-rich beef component. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed white rice in a Singaporean family meal or hawker setting. Nutritionally, the dish is relatively low in carbohydrates (3.3g/100g) and provides moderate protein (7.2g/100g) and fat (7.3g/100g), yielding 109 kcal per 100g. However, its sodium content is extremely high at 1061mg/100g, driven by the salted preserved vegetables and condiments, making it a significant concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for low-sodium diets and should be consumed in small portions. It is not vegetarian or vegan due to the beef content, and may not be halal depending on the source of beef and oyster sauce used. The glycaemic impact is minimal given the very low carbohydrate content.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database with confidence 0.95 for an exact match entry (cr_id F14080054). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework given the very low carbohydrate content of the dish.
notes: Fields set to -1 indicate data not available in the HPB source record for this dish: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, sugar_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this traditional Singaporean stir-fried dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database as of 2026. Saturated fat estimated at 2.8g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central data for comparable beef and vegetable stir-fry dishes (USDA SR Legacy, beef stir-fry with vegetables). The input record incorrectly tagged this dish as 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' in dietary_tags; these have been corrected to false as the dish contains beef. The halal status is conditionally dependent on the source of beef and oyster sauce used in preparation. GI value of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The input GI confidence of 0.365 reflects low certainty and this has been preserved in the analogous estimate classification. FoodEx2 match type corrected from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enumeration values of closest_parent, exact, and none.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected dietary_flags: is_vegetarian and is_vegan set to false (input incorrectly tagged dish as vegetarian and plant_based despite containing beef).
Added all mandatory Singaporean multilingual names: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Added romanized local name 'Xian Cai Chao Niu Rou'.
Populated saturated_fat_g with 2.8g estimated from USDA SR Legacy comparable beef stir-fry dish.
Documented all -1 fields in notes with reason and source consulted.
Replaced 'Embedded GI Database' (forbidden source) with University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate framework.
Restructured health_context to include all three required conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) with correct field structure.
Built comprehensive aliases_by_language and verified all entries appear verbatim in flat aliases array.
Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type from 'broad' to 'closest_parent' to align with allowed values.

Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enumeration values of ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] — this was the g5 failure identified in iteration 2 validator feedback.
Corrected condition key from 'diabetes' to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes'.
